# External Convection

We're going to dig deeper into convection, specifically "external convection" -- fluids passing over objects. We've hinted at the physics already, e.g.: convection is enhanced by increasing the flow rate, and water is a more effective heat transfer medium than air due to its thermal capacity. After this chapter, we'll be able to quantitatively explain why via calculations of $h$.

Fluid flow is complex and we won't be solving for temperature distributions like we did with conduction. Instead we'll focus mostly on overall heat transfer rates as described by an averaged value of the convection coefficient, $\overline{h}$. Engineers have collected experimental data and come up with a best-fit equation to describe it -- we will obtain $\overline{h}$ from these "correlations".

::: {.callout-note}
I have retrieved a lot of these correlations from Incropera, DeWitt, Bergman, and Lavine "Fundamentals of Heat and Mass Transfer", the sixth edition published in 2007. The first edition of this textbook was written by just Incropera and DeWitt, in 1974. It is very common to refer to their textbook as just "Incropera and DeWitt", as in: "this table comes from Incropera and DeWitt".
:::

## Think First

Recall

1. We've been computing convection with $\dot{Q} = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$. Before reading further: what physical factors do you expect $h$ to depend on? (Think about what changes if you blow faster, use a different fluid, or change the object's size.)
2. From our resistor analogy: what is the thermal resistance for convection? What happens to that resistance as $h$ increases?
3. From fluid mechanics: what is the Reynolds number $\text{Re} = \rho U L / \mu$, and what does a large Reynolds number indicate about a flow?

Look Ahead

1. If you blow air across a flat plate, do you expect the local heat transfer to be the same everywhere along the plate, or to vary with position? What might cause it to change?
2. Turbulent flow is more chaotic and well-mixed than laminar flow. How do you expect the laminar-to-turbulent transition in the boundary layer to affect the "local" heat transfer coefficient?
3. A cold fluid at temperature $T_\text{in}$ passes over a series of hot cylinders, say 40 of them. If you tried to compute the total amount of heat transfer as $\dot{Q} = \overline{h} A_\text{cylinder} (T_\text{cylinder} - T_\text{in})$, would that be an overestimate or underestimate, and why?

## What is $h$?

::: {.callout-tip}
### Video

Start with [this linked video](https://canvas.colorado.edu/courses/136150/pages/intro-to-convection), an introduction to convection.
:::

### Circumventing the boundary layer details

We've been using $h$ in the context of

$$
\dot{Q} = h A_\text{surface}(T_\text{surface}-T_\infty).
$$ {#eq-convection}

Flow phenomena are included within $h$, and that should make us nervous! Can you tell me the velocity profile for turbulent flow around a sphere? Somehow that is factored-in to $h$!

There is good news: $h$ is a quantity we invented to summarize the effects of flow properties, including material properties like density and viscosity. Even as we dig deeper into convection, we maintain the definition given in @eq-convection, including the use of $\Delta T = T_\text{surface}-T_\infty$. It is much simpler to just think about the surface temperature and ambient temperature than it is to deal with the detailed temperature distribution spanning those values! This is the power of $h$... it allows us to do that.

::: {.column-margin}
We will have to use a modified $\Delta T$ for heat exchangers, but the spirit is the same: to focus on inlet/outlet conditions and gloss-over the nuances of the profiles.
:::

### Local and average $h$

We've been a bit sloppy in our discussion of the convective heat transfer coefficient, saying for example that $h=22$ W/m²·K for flow over an object. More rigorously, $h$ can be described as a "local value", as in the value at a particular position on the surface; or as an "averaged value", as in an effective/averaged value over an entire surface. Technically we have been talking about an averaged value $\overline{h}$ up to this point in the course, which would be defined as 

$$
\overline{h}=\frac{1}{A_\text{surface}}\int h(\mathbf{x})dS
$$

and this is what we'd use in an overall heat transfer calculation

$$
\dot{Q}= \overline{h} {A_\text{surface}} \Delta T. 
$$

We will talk a bit about the local $h$, but when it comes to solving typical heat transfer problems, we will be jumping straight to values of $\overline{h}$ that have been cataloged by our ancestors.


### An experimentalist's approach

The convection coefficient can be determined analytically: figure out the temperature profile in the fluid, evaluate the heat flux at the surface, and rearrange

$$
q'' = -k_f \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\bigg\rvert_\text{surface} = h(T_\text{surface}-T_\infty)
$$

to find

$$
h = \frac{-k_f \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\bigg\rvert_\text{surface}}{T_\text{surface}-T_\infty}.
$$

Rather than solve the coupled nonlinear partial differential equations necessary to determine the heat flux at each point on the surface, we might instead think about how to determine the relationship between $h$ and the problem parameters using dimensional analysis. We should expect that $h$ depends on:

- The incoming flow velocity, $U_\infty$. This sets how much cool fluid is passing over the object, and also is related to boundary layers, flow separation, and turbulence.
- Also relevant to boundary layers, etc., is the fluid viscosity, $\mu$.
- The specific heat, $c_p$, which is how much energy it takes to heat up a kilogram of fluid by one Kelvin. If it takes more energy to change the temperature of the fluid, it is going to do a better job at removing heat from the object.
- Tied to that is the density: how many kilograms are passing by?
- The thermal conductivity of the fluid, $k_f$, is also relevant. Convection is ostensibly moving energy downstream, but it depends on the rate at which we can conduct energy perpendicular to the flow, away from the surface.
- Position or length is relevant, too. The distance from the leading edge, $L$, has an effect on the flow profile.

That is, we should have $h(U_\infty,\mu,c_p,\rho, k_f, L)$. 

::: {.callout-note}
Recall the Buckingham-$\Pi$ Theorem: A problem with $n$ parameters/variables, described by $r$ fundamental dimensions, is governed by at most $n-r$ dimensionless parameters.
:::

So: 7 parameters, and 4 dimensions are necessary to quantify them: mass, length, time, and temperature. Thus we should expect 3 dimensionless groups. They are:

$$
\text{The Reynolds Number:  }\text{Re}_L\equiv \frac{\rho U_\infty L}{\mu}
$$

$$
\text{The Nusselt Number:  }\text{Nu}_L\equiv \frac{h L}{k_f}
$$

$$
\text{The Prandtl Number:  }\text{Pr}\equiv \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu/\rho}{k_f/(\rho c_p)} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k_f}.
$$

The Nusselt Number is essentially a dimensionless $h$, and we will spend this week and next calculating values for it via "Nusselt Number correlations". Buckingham-$\Pi$ tells us that it is a function of two things: the Reynolds Number and the Prandtl Number.

A few notes:

- The Reynolds and Nusselt Numbers often include a subscript to denote what characteristic length is being used in the definition, e.g. for a flat plate with length $L$, we would use $\text{Re}_L = \rho U_\infty L/\mu$; for a cylinder with diameter $D$, we would use $\text{Re}_D = \rho U_\infty D/\mu$.
- The Nusselt Number looks exactly like the Biot Number: $hL/k$. The difference is that the Nusselt Number uses the thermal conductivity of the fluid, the Biot Number uses the thermal conductivity of the solid.
- We might seek an averaged $\overline{h}$, in which case we'd use an averaged $\overline{\text{Nu}}$. This is more directly useful in determining the overall heat transfer: $\dot{Q}=\overline{h} A \Delta T$.
- The Prandtl Number is a material property that can be looked up just like the thermal conductivity. Its value can be highly dependent on temperature: for water at 5°C, $\text{Pr}=11.2$; at 95°C, $\text{Pr}=1.9$. We still will treat it as a constant.

A quick example of a Nusselt Number correlation: for flow over a cylinder we might use

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 0.3 + \frac{0.62\,\text{Re}_D^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{\left[1+(0.4/\text{Pr})^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}}\left[1+\left(\frac{\text{Re}_D}{282000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}
$$

Then...

### The Algorithm for Solving Convection Problems

1. Identify your model geometry and look up the relevant Nusselt Number correlation. 
2. Retrieve all necessary fluid properties. It is common to use "the film temperature" as the reference temperature: $T_\text{film} \equiv (T_\infty + T_\text{surface})/2$. Often there is a parameter with a subscript $s$, as in @eq-sphere-corr -- this parameter is to be evaluated at the surface temperature. (Sometimes the correlations are defined based on another reference temperature -- just be on the lookout!)
3. Calculate the relevant Reynolds Number and use it, along with the Prandtl Number, in the Nusselt Number correlation. You will get a value, e.g. $\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 11.2$.
4. Unpack the Nusselt Number correlation: $\overline{\text{Nu}}_L \equiv \overline{h}L/k$ means $\overline{h} = (k/L) \overline{\text{Nu}}_L$.
5. Use $\overline{h}$ in the convective cooling law: $\dot{Q} = \overline{h}A_\text{surface}(T_\text{surface}-T_\infty)$.


## The Flat Plate


::: {.callout-tip}
### Video

Start with [this linked video](https://canvas.colorado.edu/courses/136150/pages/flat-plate-heat-transfer) on the flat-plate geometry.
:::

### The geometry

A uniform horizontal flow with speed $U_\infty$ and temperature $T_\infty$ approaches a horizontal flat plate. Due to friction at the plate, a boundary layer structure will form, with an associated velocity distribution that spans from zero (due to the no-slip condition) up to $U_\infty$ far from the plate. We will similarly have a "thermal" boundary layer, where the temperature distribution spans from a surface value $T_s$ to $T_\infty$ far from the plate. (At least that is the case for an "isothermal" plate. We also will consider the situation when $q''_s$ is a constant, and there will be a boundary layer structure there too.)

### The momentum boundary layer thickness

We know convection is going to remove heat from an object, so somehow the velocity profile affects the temperature profile...

You may recall from fluid mechanics that we can use dimensional analysis to determine the dependence of the boundary layer thickness, $\delta$, on the problem parameters. A reminder: we have a no-slip condition at the plate's surface that sets up a competition between "advection", the flow going downstream characterized by $U_\infty$, and viscous diffusion, the friction between neighboring parcels traveling at different speeds characterized by $\nu\equiv \mu/\rho$. The boundary layer grows as we move further from the leading edge, distance given by $L$, and so we should expect $\delta(L,U_\infty,\nu)$. Check the dimensions of this scaling equation:

$$
\delta \sim \sqrt{\frac{\nu L}{U_\infty}}.
$$

The physics described here makes sense: the boundary layer grows with $L$, and will grow more rapidly if it is a more diffusive material. If the flow is faster, there will be less time to diffuse before reaching a distance $L$... the thickness should decrease with increasing $U_\infty$. All good. This relation can be rearranged to:

$$
\frac{\delta}{L} \sim \sqrt{\frac{\nu }{U_\infty L}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\text{Re}_L}}.
$$

### The thermal boundary layer thickness

We have a similar competition for the thermal boundary layer, thickness $\delta_T$: now it's a thermal diffusivity, $\alpha$, competing with advection. The diffusivities have the same dimensions, (length)²/(time), and so we expect the same dimensional relationship between the parameters:

$$
\delta_T \sim \sqrt{\frac{\alpha L}{U_\infty}}
$$

or 

$$
\frac{\delta_T}{L} \sim \sqrt{\frac{\alpha }{U_\infty L}} = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\nu}\frac{\nu }{U_\infty L}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\text{Pr}\cdot\text{Re}_L}}.
$$

Observe:

$$
\frac{\delta_T}{\delta} = 1/\sqrt{\text{Pr}}.
$$

So the Prandtl Number tells us the ratio between the momentum and thermal boundary layer thicknesses.

<!-- 
### Boundary layer equations

If we scale the 

The non-dimensionalized (fluid) boundary layer equation is:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}+u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}+v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}}\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}
$$

and the thermal boundary layer equation is:



 (The first of these equations comes from conservation of momentum, the second from conservation of energy.) Notice that these equations have exactly the same form, with the same boundary conditions. The only difference: a coefficient of $1/\text{Re}$ in one, and $1/(\text{Re}\cdot\text{Pr})$ in the other. -->


### Numerical solutions

After non-dimensionalizing the equations (in a way that includes $\delta$ and $\delta_T$), we can arrive at: the conservation of $x$-momentum:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}+u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}+v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}}\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}
$$

and the conservation of energy

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t}+u \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x}+v\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\text{Re}\cdot\text{Pr}}\frac{\partial^2\theta}{\partial y^2}.
$$

with $u(0)=0$, $u(\infty)=1$ and $\theta(0)=0$, $\theta(\infty)=1$. These equations are not solvable analytically, but we can solve them numerically. (If you took MCEN 3030 with me, you solved the first one in a different form, after plugging-in conservation of mass.) So we can determine a numerical value for the velocity gradient at the surface, which is related to the viscous drag, and can determine a numerical value for the temperature gradient at the surface, which is related to the heat flux into the fluid.
<!-- 
Thinking back to fluid mechanics and Newton's Law of Viscosity, we can relate the shear stress at the surface $\tau_\text{surface}$ to the viscosity $\mu$ and velocity gradient at the surface:
$$
\tau_\text{surface} = \mu\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_0 \approx \mu\frac{U_\infty - 0}{\delta}.
$$

Here, we also gave an order-of-magnitude estimate of the value, based on the fact that: over the boundary layer thickness $\delta$, we will see a change in velocity of $U_\infty-0$.

We can do something similar with convection, using Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$
h(T_\text{surface}-T_\infty) = -k_f\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\bigg|_0 \approx k_f\frac{T_\infty - T_\text{surf}}{\delta_T}.
$$

Using $\delta = L\sqrt{1/\text{Re}_L}$ and $\delta_T = L\sqrt{1/(\text{Pr}\cdot\text{Re}_L)}$, we can write

$$
\tau_\text{surface} = \mu\frac{U_\infty}{L}\sqrt{\text{Re}} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \frac{h}{k_f} = \frac{\sqrt{\text{Re}\cdot\text{Pr}}}{L}.
$$

From the friction coefficient $C_f \equiv \frac{\tau_\text{surf}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2}$ (non-dimensional, used to determine drag on wall):

$$C_f = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\text{Re}_L}}$$

$$\text{Nu}_L \equiv \frac{hL}{k_f} = \sqrt{\text{Re}_L \cdot \text{Pr}}$$

Therefore $\text{Nu} = \frac{C_f}{2}\text{Re}\cdot\sqrt{\text{Pr}}$, where $C_f$ is well-documented from numerical calculations.
 -->


### Flat Plate Correlations

::: {.column-margin}
A result from fluid mechanics that we calculate numerically: we define the dimensionless coefficient of friction as 

$$
C_f(x) \equiv \frac{\tau_\text{surface}(x)}{\frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2}
$$

and, after numerically determining the velocity profile in the boundary layer, we can determine the shear stress at the surface and can write it in terms of $C_f$ and $\text{Re}_x$:

$$
C_f(x) = 0.664\text{Re}_x^{-1/2}.
$$

This is the value of the coefficient of friction at a particular $x$-value -- in order to get the total drag for $x=0$ to $x=L$, we'd need to do an integration.
:::

After solving for $T(y)$ in the boundary layer, we can determine the heat flux at the surface and can relate that to the local Nusselt Number: 

$$
\text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

where $\text{Re}_x \equiv \rho U_\infty x/\mu$. To emphasize: this is a local Nusselt Number at a particular $x$-location. Evaluating it gives us just the Nusselt Number at a particular value of $x$. To determine the convection up to this point, we need to integrate. 

The correlation changes whenever the flow transitions to turbulence: 
$$
\text{Nu}_x = 0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

and the critical Reynolds Number (where we transition to turbulence) is typically taken to be $\text{Re}_\text{crit}\approx 5\times 10^5$. (See the interactive example link below.) Both of these correlations are for constant surface temperature and Prandtl Numbers between 0.6 and 60. For a constant heat flux boundary condition:

::: {.column-margin}
The critical Reynolds Number depends on geometry: for the flat plate, it is $\text{Re}_{L,\text{crit}}\approx 5\times 10^5$, for pipe flow, it is $\text{Re}_{D,\text{crit}}\approx 2300$.
:::

$$
\text{Nu}_x = 0.453\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

for laminar flow, and

$$
\text{Nu}_x = 0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

for turbulent flow.

## Interactive Example

I created a demo that shows the dependence of $h$ on material parameters, speed, and length, highlighting the transition to turbulence. [Find it at this link](demos/flat_plate_demo.qmd). This also includes a worked example that spans the transition to turbulence.



<!-- ## Worked Example — Heated Resistors on a Flat Plate

Suppose we have a surface being heated 

*Diagram: Flow $U_\infty = 60$ m/s, $T_\infty = 25°C$ over a plate covered with resistors each 5 cm long, $T_s = 230°C$, $w = 1$ m.*

**Q:** Amount of energy needed to keep $T_s = \text{const}$? Which heater requires the most energy?

**1.** Flat plate boundary layer model — involves Nu, Re, Pr. Fluid properties: $k$, $\nu$ (itself a material property).

**2.** Properties evaluated at $T_\text{ref} = \frac{T_\infty + T_s}{2}$:
$$k = 0.0337 \;\text{W/m·K}, \quad \text{Pr} = 0.690, \quad \nu = 26.4\times10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**3.** Calculate Re:
$$\text{Re}_x = \frac{U_\infty x}{\nu} = \frac{60\;\text{m/s}}{26.4\times10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}} x = 2.27\times10^6\, x \quad (x\text{ in m})$$

$$\text{Re}_\text{crit,x} = 5\times10^5 = 2.27\times10^6\,x_\text{crit} \;\Rightarrow\; x_\text{crit} = 0.22\;\text{m}$$

**Predict:** Most power supply goes to the first resistor (leading edge), fifth resistor (includes $x_\text{crit}$), and sixth resistor (first fully turbulent).

**4.** Use local correlations:
$$\text{laminar:} \quad \text{Nu}_x = 0.332\,\text{Re}^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$
$$\text{turbulent:} \quad \text{Nu}_x = 0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{h}(x) = \frac{1}{L_\text{crit}}\int_0^{L_\text{crit}} h_\text{lam}(x)\,dx + \frac{1}{x-L_\text{crit}}\int_{L_\text{crit}}^{x} h_\text{turb}(x)\,dx$$

For the **first resistor** ($x_1 = 5$ cm), end of resistor 1:

$$\overline{h}(x_1) = \frac{1}{x_1}\int_0^{x_1} 0.332\left(\frac{U_\infty x}{\nu}\right)^{1/2}\!\text{Pr}^{1/3}\cdot\frac{k}{x}\,dx = \frac{k}{x_1}\cdot 0.664\cdot\text{Re}_{x_1}^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}$$

$$= \frac{0.0337}{0.05}\cdot(0.664)\cdot\sqrt{\frac{60\cdot(0.05)}{26.4\times10^{-6}}}\cdot(0.690)^{1/3} = 133\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

For the **fifth resistor** (spans $x_4$ to $x_\text{crit}$ laminar, $x_\text{crit}$ to $x_5$ turbulent):

$$\overline{h}_5 = \frac{k}{x_\text{crit}-x_4}\left[0.664\,\text{Re}_{x_\text{crit}}^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3} - 0.664\,\text{Re}_{x_4}^{1/2}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}\right]$$

$$+ \frac{1}{x_5 - x_\text{crit}}\int_{x_\text{crit}}^{x_5}0.0296\,\text{Re}_x^{4/5}\cdot\text{Pr}^{1/3}\cdot\frac{k}{x}\,dx$$

$$= \frac{0.0337}{0.05}\!\left[(0.664)\sqrt{5\times10^5}\cdot(0.690)^{1/3} - (0.664)\sqrt{\frac{60\cdot(0.20)}{26.4\times10^{-6}}}\,(0.690)^{1/3}\right] = 13.0\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

(laminar portion only; turbulent integral adds $143\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$)

$\Rightarrow$ for fifth resistor, $\overline{h}_5 = 156\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, this is bigger than $\overline{h}_1 = 133\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

For the **sixth resistor** (fully turbulent, $x_5$ to $x_6 = 0.30$ m):

$$\overline{h}_6 = \frac{0.0337}{0.05}\cdot(0.0296)(0.690)^{1/3}\left(\frac{60}{26.4\times10^{-6}}\right)^{4/5}\cdot\int_{0.25}^{0.30} x^{-1/5}\,dx = 138\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\therefore \quad \overline{h}_5 > \overline{h}_6 > \overline{h}_1$$ -->


## External Flow — The Cylinder

::: {.callout-tip}
### Video

Start with [this linked video](https://canvas.colorado.edu/courses/136150/pages/flow-over-cylinder) on heat transfer over cylinders.
:::

### Flow over blunt objects

For flow over blunt objects, flow separation and wakes complicate the description of heat transfer. It is very interesting to think about the local $h$ or $\text{Nu}$ as a function of position on a surface, but practically we use averaged values of $\overline{h}$ and $\overline{\text{Nu}}$ in our calculations.

::: {.callout-tip}
There are often several options for the correlations. The choice is often: use a complicated expression, and it applies to a wider range of Reynolds Number; or use a simpler expression, but it applies to a narrower range. If you are certain your design falls within a narrow $\text{Re}$ range, it is likely fine to stick with the simpler expression.
:::


From Incropera and DeWitt, a simple correlation is:

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D \equiv \frac{\overline{h}D}{k} = C\,\text{Re}_D^m\,\text{Pr}^{1/3} \qquad \text{for Pr}\geq 0.7
$$

where the values of $C$ and $m$ depend on the Reynolds Number:

| $\text{Re}_D$ | $C$ | $m$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.4–4 | 0.989 | 0.330 |
| 4–40 | 0.911 | 0.385 |
| 40–4000 | 0.683 | 0.466 |
| 4000–40000 | 0.193 | 0.618 |
| 40000–400000 | 0.027 | 0.805 |

(All properties evaluated at $T_\text{film} = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$.)



A slight improvement in accuracy is achieved with this modification:

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = C\cdot\text{Re}_D^m\cdot\text{Pr}^n\cdot\left(\frac{\text{Pr}}{\text{Pr}_s}\right)^{1/4} \qquad \text{(Pr}_s\text{ evaluated at }T_s\text{)}$$

with $C$ and $m$ from above, and

$$n = \begin{cases}0.36 & \text{if Pr}>10 \\ 0.37 & \text{if Pr}\leq10\end{cases}$$

Or, more complicated but valid for all $\text{Re}_D$ (cylinder):

$$\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 0.3 + \frac{0.62\,\text{Re}_D^{1/2}\,\text{Pr}^{1/3}}{\left[1+(0.4/\text{Pr})^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}}\left[1+\left(\frac{\text{Re}_D}{282000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$$


::: {.callout-note}
In the limit that $\text{Re}_D \to 0$ ("creeping flow"):

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 0.3 = \frac{\overline{h}D}{k}
$$

That is: $\overline{h}$ increases for smaller objects.
:::



## External Flow — The Sphere

The same physics applies to the sphere as well. The correlation is:

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 2 + \left(0.4\,\text{Re}_D^{1/2} + 0.06\,\text{Re}_D^{2/3}\right)\text{Pr}^{0.4}\cdot\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_s}\right)^{1/4}
$$ {#eq-sphere-corr}

**All properties evaluated at** $T_\infty$(!) except $\mu_s$ which is evaluated at the surface temperature.



### Worked Example — Copper Sphere Cooling

A copper sphere with diameter 10 mm begins at 75°C and is to be cooled to 35°C by air. The air has speed 10 m/s and temperature 23°C.

We look up the properties of copper at 55°C: $k = 399$ W/m·K, $c = 388$ J/kg·K, $\rho = 8933$ kg/m³.

For the sphere correlation, @eq-sphere-corr, most of the fluid properties are evaluated at $T_\infty$: $\mu = 182.6\times 10^{-7}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1.17$ kg/m³, $\text{Pr} = 0.708$, and $k = 0.0251$ W/m·K. We also need the fluid viscosity evaluated at the surface temperature, and we use an average 55°C: $\mu_s = 197.8\times10^{-7}$ Pa·s.

Start with calculating the Reynolds Number:

$$
\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho U_\infty D}{\mu} = 6440
$$

Then use the correlation from above to get

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = 47.1 = \frac{\overline{h}D}{k_f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \overline{h} = 118\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

Check Bi:

$$\text{Bi} = \frac{\overline{h}\cdot D/2}{k_\text{copper}} = 0.0015 < 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LC model is good}$$

Apply lumped capacitance:

$$\theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(\frac{-t}{t_c}\right), \qquad t_c \equiv \frac{mc}{\overline{h}A_\text{surf}}$$

At $T = T_f = 35°C$, $\theta = \frac{35-23}{75-23}=0.231$, which means $t/t_c = 1.466$. With

$$
m = \rho \frac{\pi D^3}{6} = 8933\times\frac{\pi(0.01)^3}{6} = 4.68\times 10^{-3}\text{ kg}
$$

and

$$
A_\text{surf} = \pi D^2 = 3.14\times10^{-4}\text{ m}^2
$$

$$
t_c = \frac{mc}{\bar h A_s} = \frac{4.68\times10^{-3}\times388}{118\times3.14\times10^{-4}} = \frac{1.816}{0.0371} = 48.9\text{ s}
$$

and so $t=71.7$ s.

## Flow Over Banks of Tubes

::: {.callout-tip}
### Video

Start with [this linked video](https://canvas.colorado.edu/courses/136150/pages/arrays-of-tubes) on the heat transfer over banks of tubes.
:::



### Nusselt Number Correlation for Tube Banks

How about a more practical geometry? One of the most important applications of heat transfer is the heat exchanger (covered in detail later), and one of the most common designs is the shell-and-tube. With this type of device, we basically pump cold fluid over a collection of hot pipes -- sounds similar to the flow over the cylinder description from above, but in this case we can't say that every cylinder is experiencing a flow with $U_\infty$ and $T_\infty$... it is much more complicated.

::: {.column-margin}
It is very common to see a shell-and-tube heat exchanger as a condenser in the Rankine Cycle, responsible for turning steam into a liquid. Remember that, during a phase change, water maintains a constant temperature -- it is not at all unreasonable to assume that $T_\text{s}$ (the pipe surface temperature) is a constant in this application.
:::

Our hope is to find a $\overline{\text{Nu}}_D$ that describes the entire array, and here's one that is deceptively simple:


$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = C_1\,\text{Re}_{D,\max}^m\cdot\text{Pr}^{0.36}\cdot\left(\frac{\text{Pr}}{\text{Pr}_s}\right)^{1/4}
$$

::: {.column-margin}
Properties evaluated at $T_\text{avg} = \frac{T_\text{in}+T_\text{out}}{2}$ except for $\text{Pr}_s$, evaluated at the surface temperature. But how do we know $T_\text{out}$? More on that soon...
:::

This applies to situations in which:
- the number of rows of cylinders $N_L \geq 20$
- the Reynolds Number (see definition below) is $1000 \leq \text{Re}_{D,\max} \leq 2\times10^6$
- and the Prandtl Number is $0.7 \leq \text{Pr} \leq 500$.

<!-- ![Aligned tube bank geometry, showing longitudinal pitch $S_L$, transverse pitch $S_T$, tube diameter $D$, and minimum flow area $A_1$.](images/tube_bank_aligned.svg){width=70%} -->


The cylinders are said to be "aligned" when the second row is directly behind the first row, from the perspective of the incoming flow. That is, the incoming flow can't "see" the second row. The cylinders are "staggered" if the second row is offset from the first.

We'll need to define a few geometric parameters. From the perspective of the incoming flow...
- $S_T$ is the left-to-right distance between the cylinders in the first row (center to center)
- $N_T$ is the number of cylinders in the first row
- $S_L$ is the distance between the first and second row (distance from the line connecting the first row centers to the line connecting the second row centers)
- $N_L$ is the number of rows
- and of course $D$ is the cylinder diameter.

The Reynolds Number is based on the highest flow rate $V_\text{max}$. In the aligned case:

$$
V_\text{max} = \frac{S_T}{S_T - D}V_\infty.
$$

In the staggered case: if

$$
S_D \equiv \left[S_L^2 + \left(\frac{S_T}{2}\right)^2\right]^{1/2} < \frac{S_T+D}{2}
$$

then 

$$
V_\text{max} = \frac{S_T}{2(S_D-D)}V_\infty.
$$

Else, we use the same expression as the aligned case. Then

$$
\text{Re}_{D,\text{max}}\equiv \frac{\rho V_\text{max}D}{\mu}.
$$

With all of that defined, here is a table reproduced from Incropera and DeWitt (Table 7.5):



| Configuration | $\text{Re}_{D,\max}$ | $C_1$ | $m$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aligned | $10$–$10^2$ | 0.80 | 0.40 |
| Staggered | $10$–$10^2$ | 0.90 | 0.40 |
| Aligned / Staggered | $10^2$–$10^3$ | Approximate as a single (isolated) cylinder | |
| Aligned $(S_T/S_L > 0.7)$ | $10^3$–$2\times10^5$ | 0.27 | 0.63 |
| Staggered $(S_T/S_L < 2)$ | $10^3$–$2\times10^5$ | $0.35(S_T/S_L)^{1/5}$ | 0.60 |
| Staggered $(S_T/S_L > 2)$ | $10^3$–$2\times10^5$ | 0.40 | 0.60 |
| Aligned | $2\times10^5$–$2\times10^6$ | 0.021 | 0.84 |
| Staggered | $2\times10^5$–$2\times10^6$ | 0.022 | 0.84 |

### Correction factor for few rows

For $N_L < 20$ we use the same expression as above, with a correction factor:

$$
\overline{\text{Nu}}_D = C_2\,C_1\,\text{Re}_{D,\max}^m\cdot\text{Pr}^{0.36}\cdot\left(\frac{\text{Pr}}{\text{Pr}_s}\right)^{1/4}.
$$

Values of $C_2$:

| $N_L$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 7 | 10 | 13 | 16 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aligned | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.86 | 0.90 | 0.92 | 0.95 | 0.97 | 0.98 | 0.99 |
| Staggered | 0.64 | 0.76 | 0.84 | 0.89 | 0.92 | 0.95 | 0.97 | 0.98 | 0.99 |

---

### The Log-Mean Temperature Difference

When it comes to predicting total heat transfer, it is **not** the case that $\dot{Q}$ simply scales with $N$ and $\Delta T=T_\text{surf}-T_\infty$. Instead, use the log-mean temperature difference:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{(T_s - T_\text{in}) - (T_s - T_\text{out})}{\ln\!\left(\frac{T_s - T_\text{in}}{T_s - T_\text{out}}\right)}
$$

... a better representation of the average local $\Delta T$. The external flow's inlet temperature is $T_\text{in}$ and the outlet temperature is $T_\text{out}$. Of course, we don't know what the outlet temperature is, exactly! Knowing that requires us to know how much heat transfer has occurred, and that is the whole problem!

::: {.column-margin}
The numerator of $\Delta T_{lm}$ can indeed be simplified to $T_\text{out}-T_\text{in}$. But we will use the log-mean temperature difference later, and more generally it has a "two differences" structure -- so we write it in terms of two differences here.
:::


We have a way of estimating $T_\text{out}$ though:


$$
\frac{T_s - T_\text{out}}{T_s - T_\text{in}} \approx \exp\!\left(\frac{-\pi D N \overline{h}}{\rho V_\infty N_T S_T c_p}\right)
$$

where $N=N_T\cdot N_L$ is the total number of tubes.



### Calculating the heat transfer

We finally arrive at an expression for the total amount of heat transfer over the bank of tubes:

$$
\dot{Q} = N\overline{h}\,\pi DL\cdot\Delta T_{lm}
$$


## Interactive Demo -- LMTD

I created an interactive demo to explore the log-mean temperature difference. [Find it at this link](demos/LMTD_demo.qmd).
